items.q: Why did the American colonies rebel against Britain?
items.correct: Disputes over taxation without representation after the Seven Years' War, growing colonial identity, and British attempts to tighten imperial control on previously autonomous colonies.
items.misconception: The colonists rebelled because they were heavily oppressed and impoverished.
items.random_wrong: Because Britain banned them from owning land west of the Atlantic.
items.why: Americans were actually among the wealthiest and least-taxed people in the empire. The revolt was about principle and political control, not material suffering — a key counter-intuitive point.

items.correct: A combination of moral campaigning, slave resistance and rebellions (especially Haiti and Jamaica), and the shifting economic interest of industrial capitalism.
items.misconception: Wilberforce single-handedly persuaded Parliament to end slavery out of pure morality.
items.random_wrong: Slavery was abolished because cotton was no longer profitable.
items.why: Students inherit the heroic Wilberforce narrative. Recent scholarship emphasises slave revolts (Haiti, Demerara, Jamaica) and economic shifts — abolition was not a gift from above.

items.q: Why is 1688 called the Glorious Revolution?
items.correct: Parliament invited William of Orange to replace the Catholic James II, with little bloodshed in England, establishing parliamentary supremacy and the 1689 Bill of Rights.
items.misconception: It was glorious because no one died anywhere in the conflict.
items.random_wrong: William of Orange was Charles II's son and inherited the throne.
items.why: It was bloody in Ireland and Scotland (Boyne, Killiecrankie). Students take the English-centric label literally and miss the violent imposition of the new regime elsewhere.

items.q: Who led the first Labour government in 1924?
items.why: It's Ramsay MacDonald. “Clement Attlee” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many days did the General Strike of 1926 last?
items.why: It's 9. “21” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did women gain the vote on equal terms with men?
items.why: Exact answer: 1928. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
